title: Ration Court Count
desc: The mid-level ration court is between shifts, which gives you five calm minutes to fix the numbers before it doesn't.

steps.text:
Danian order is easiest to admire from a distance. Up close it involves vats, ladles, attendance slates, and workers who want their fungus cakes before the next bell. The mid-level ration court serves four labor corridors and one training lane. By the time you arrive, the portion blocks are stacked, the mineral mash is warm, and the count slate says one thing while the trays say another.

The meal recorder taps the mismatch with one claw. "Either the cutting bench is short six portions, or the upper carry rack took more than it signed for," she says. "Recount and recut the slab here, or follow the carry route and see where the extra meals actually went. Do it before the next shift arrives and turns arithmetic into a queue."

options.label: Recount the portions and recut the slab at the bench
Follow the carry route and track the missing portions

steps.text:
The cutting bench is lined with firm fungus slabs, each marked for standard portions by a wire grid the recorder swears is never wrong. She is almost right. The grid is true, but the last cutter took the end row too narrow after one side of the slab dried and shrank. Six pieces that should have been full portions are only nearly so, which matters when the whole court is counted by body and task.

You recut a fresh section from the reserve slab, add measured scoops of mineral mash to the corrected trays, and watch the numbers settle back into agreement. The recorder checks three times anyway because that is the kind of creature she is. When the first line of workers comes in, every tray is ready and every mark on the slate matches what leaves the counter. She makes one satisfied note and says, "Good. Hunger creates arguments. Proper counting prevents them."

options.label: Set the reserve slab in place and open the line

steps.text:
The carry route loops from the ration court up one ramp, through a side junction, and into the training lane where younger workers and drill groups eat between assignments. You follow the rack and find the error before you reach the end. One of the upper trays has two chalk marks where it should have one, the sort of smear that looks obvious only after it has already sent six portions in the wrong direction.

The lane attendant is not embarrassed so much as annoyed at the chalk for being wet enough to blur in the first place. Together you restack the extra meals, correct the tray marks, and send the surplus back down before the next line forms. It is a small mistake, but in Mount Pillar small mistakes reproduce quickly if left alone. By the time you return to the ration court, the recorder has the corrected slate ready and the court is opening on schedule. She glances at the extra tray, then at your explanation, and says, "Excellent. We have prevented confusion from becoming routine."

options.label: Put the tray back in line and let the count hold
